issues.issue: Meta-commentary about source availability addresses the production process rather than the reader.
issues.quote: though it was not available to this project for direct consultation
issues.suggestion: Remove the clause or rephrase to avoid meta-commentary, e.g., 'The English-language grammar <Ref k="tamura2000" /> made the Saru description accessible to non-Japanese readership.'

issues.issue: This phrase uses first-person plural possessive ('this project') and refers to internal access limitations — a register leak from the production pipeline. The grammar otherwise maintains an impersonal scholarly voice.
issues.quote: though it was not available to this project for direct consultation
issues.suggestion: Rephrase to: 'though it was not available for direct consultation'

issues.issue: The table lists Nakagawa (2024) as an 'extensive grammar', but the text refers to it as 'An extensive grammar of the Ainu language' (line 337).
issues.quote: Nakagawa
issues.suggestion: Ensure consistency in the description of Nakagawa's work.

issues.issue: The table includes Nakagawa 2024 as a source, but in the prose (line 337), Nakagawa's work is cited as <Ref k="nakagawa2024" /> — implying it is available. However, on line 343, the grammar states 'A critical review of its analyses and relation to earlier grammars is available in <Ref k="sato2025b" />' — a 2025 source. Since this is July 2026, a 2025 review is plausible, but the table includes a 2024 source without noting any potential availability issues. No problem per se, but the prose on line 160 says 'though it was not available to this project for direct consultation' regarding Tamura 2000 — yet no such note is made for Nakagawa 2024, which is more recent. This creates an internal inconsistency in citation transparency.
issues.suggestion: Add a note clarifying whether Nakagawa 2024 was directly consulted or not, to match the transparency of the Tamura 2000 statement.

issues.issue: The table lists '1905 / 1938' for Batchelor, but the text mentions 1905 and 1938 editions separately (lines 51-52).
issues.quote: Batchelor
issues.suggestion: Clarify the table entry to reflect the distinct editions mentioned in the text.

issues.issue: In the table, the year entry uses a spaced slash (' / '), while other multi-year entries (e.g., line 126) use an en dash. This creates format drift in date presentation.
issues.suggestion: Use an en dash: '1905–1938'

issues.issue: Slight awkwardness due to repeated 'and' in close succession: '...glossaries for practical administrative use. The most comprehensive of these... contains... and short texts, and it served...'
issues.quote: and short texts, and it served
issues.suggestion: Replace with: '...and short texts; it served'

issues.issue: The prefixes are italicized and tagged with lang="ain-Latn", but the spacing and punctuation are inconsistent: there is a space after each comma, but the final 'and' is in plain text. For typographic consistency, the list should be uniformly formatted.
issues.quote: e-, ko-, and o-
issues.suggestion: Ensure consistent spacing and formatting: 'e-, ko-, and o-' is acceptable, but confirm uniform italics and language tagging.

issues.issue: The same citation key 'dalcorso2021' is used for two different re-editions (Piłsudski texts and Murasaki grammar), which may be ambiguous if the reference list has only one entry.
issues.quote: re-edited with translation and grammatical notes by <Ref k="dalcorso2021" /> (line 70) and later re-edited and annotated by <Ref k="dalcorso2021" /> (line 142)
issues.suggestion: Ensure the reference list has a single entry for dalcorso2021 that covers both re-editions, or use distinct keys if they are separate works.

issues.issue: Dialect tag uses an HTML span with class 'sc' instead of the guillemet tags (e.g., ‹SA›) described in the briefing as the book's convention.
issues.quote: marked with the dialect tag sa
issues.suggestion: Replace with guillemet tag ‹SA› for consistency with the book's style.

issues.issue: Meta-commentary about source edition addresses the production process rather than the reader.
issues.quote: held in this project's sources in the <Ref k="batchelor1905" /> edition and in its final revised form of <Ref k="batchelor1938" />
issues.suggestion: Rephrase to 'His dictionary — in the <Ref k="batchelor1905" /> edition and its final revised form of <Ref k="batchelor1938" /> — combined...'

issues.issue: Meta-commentary about the chapter's sources addresses the production process rather than the reader.
issues.quote: large parts of the historical account in the present chapter draw on that survey
issues.suggestion: Rephrase to 'The historical account in this chapter draws heavily on that survey.'

issues.issue: Meta-commentary about the grammar's methodology addresses the production process rather than the reader.
issues.quote: against which this grammar triangulates its analyses
issues.suggestion: Rephrase to 'against which the present grammar is triangulated' or simply 'Together with Tamura's Saru dictionary and grammar, it forms one of the two principal Japanese-language reference works for Ainu.'

issues.issue: The English translation is not in title case, while other translated titles (e.g., line 103) use sentence-style capitalization. Inconsistent capitalization style for translated titles.

issues.suggestion: Use title case: 'An Extensive Grammar of the Ainu Language'

issues.issue: The Japanese title is correctly given, but the English translation uses lowercase 'grammar' in 'An outline of Ainu grammar' — standard title case would capitalize 'Grammar'.
issues.quote: アイヌ語法概説 ('An outline of Ainu grammar')
issues.suggestion: Use title case: 'An Outline of Ainu Grammar'

issues.issue: Hyphenated compound 'Saru-dialect' is used once, but elsewhere the unhyphenated 'Saru dialect' or similar forms are used (e.g., line 149). Inconsistent hyphenation of the same term within the chapter.
issues.quote: Saru-dialect
issues.suggestion: Use 'Saru dialect' consistently without hyphenation.

issues.issue: Ambiguous pronoun 'he' after 'Refsing' – the antecedent is Kindaichi, not Refsing.
issues.quote: Refsing identifies two advances in particular: he demonstrated that the language's person-indexing elements are personal affixes on the verb rather than the free pronouns that Batchelor's description had posited, and he distinguished the abstract nominal form from the personal-reference-bearing concrete (possessed) form
issues.suggestion: Change 'he demonstrated' to 'Kindaichi demonstrated' and 'he distinguished' to 'Kindaichi distinguished' to clarify the antecedent.
